## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**(3 Marks)**
*Distinguish between step-growth and chain-growth polymerisation with respect to (i) chain initiation, (ii) molecular weight build-up, and (iii) presence/absence of byproducts.*

**Model Answer:**
(i) **Initiation:** Step-growth does **not** require a separate initiator; monomers themselves carry reactive functional groups. Chain-growth **requires** an external initiator (thermal/radical/ionic) to generate the active centre.

(ii) **Molecular weight build-up:** In step-growth, high molecular weight is achieved only at very high conversion (≥ 98%); the number-average DP increases slowly until near the end. In chain-growth, high molecular weight is reached **early** in the reaction because the chain grows rapidly after initiation.

(iii) **Byproducts:** Step-growth **condensation** polymerisation releases small molecules (H₂O, HCl, etc.); chain-growth polymerisation releases **no** byproduct.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**(3 Marks)**
*State the Carothers equation. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_{n}$ at 95% conversion for a stoichiometric step-growth polymerisation.*

**Model Answer:**
The Carothers equation for a stoichiometric step-growth system is:

$$\overline{X}_{n} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

Given $p = 0.95$:

$$\overline{X}_{n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

Therefore, the number-average DP at 95% conversion is **20**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *With the help of a neat kinetic scheme, derive the rate expression for free-radical chain-growth polymerisation. State the steady-state assumption used.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *For a free-radical polymerisation, the rate of polymerisation is observed to be first-order in monomer concentration and half-order in initiator concentration. Justify this observation from the derived rate expression. If the rate constant of termination is doubled, how will the rate of polymerisation change?*

##### **Model Solution (a):**

The three-stage free-radical scheme is:

**1. Initiation**

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 R^{\bullet} \qquad \text{(Decomposition)}$$

$$R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} RM^{\bullet} \qquad \text{(Initiation)}$$

Rate of initiation: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$

**2. Propagation** (the dominant step)

$$RM^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_{2}^{\bullet}$$
$$RM_{n}^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

Rate of propagation: $R_p = k_p [M][M^{\bullet}]$

**3. Termination**

$$RM_{n}^{\bullet} + RM_{m}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{Dead Polymer (combination or disproportionation)}$$

Rate of termination: $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$

**Steady-State Assumption:**
The concentration of active radicals is constant with time:

$$\frac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0$$

Therefore:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

Substituting back into $R_p$:

$$\boxed{R_{p} = k_p \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}} \cdot [M]}$$

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Justification of observed orders:**
- **First-order in [M]:** From the derived expression, $R_p \propto [M]^1$. ✅
- **Half-order in [I]:** From the derived expression, $R_p \propto [I]^{0.5}$. ✅

**Effect of doubling $k_t$:**
Since $R_p \propto k_t^{-0.5}$, doubling $k_t$ changes $R_p$ by a factor of:

$$\frac{R_p^{\text{new}}}{R_p^{\text{old}}} = \sqrt{\frac{k_t^{\text{old}}}{2 k_t^{\text{old}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

**Result:** The rate of polymerisation becomes approximately **70.7% of the original** (i.e., decreases by ~ 29.3%).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the mechanism of anionic ring-opening polymerisation of ε-caprolactone. Discuss the role of ring strain as the thermodynamic driving force.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A step-growth polymerisation is carried out with stoichiometric imbalance $r = 0.98$. Calculate the maximum $\overline{X}_{n}$ achievable. Comment on the industrial significance of stoichiometric imbalance in polymer design.*

##### **Model Solution (a):**

**Mechanism of Anionic ROP of ε-Caprolactone:**

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
The initiator (e.g., potassium tert-butoxide, $KO^tBu$) provides an alkoxide anion ($RO^-$). The alkoxide attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of ε-caprolactone:

$$RO^- + \overset{O}{C}-(CH_2)_5 \longrightarrow RO-\overset{O}{C}-(CH_2)_5-O^-$$

The 7-membered ring **opens** with relief of ring strain.

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The newly generated alkoxide attacks the next ε-caprolactone ring:

$$RO-(\text{caprolactone unit})-O^- + \text{caprolactone} \longrightarrow \text{extended alkoxide}$$

This step repeats n times.

**Step 3 — Termination:**
Protonation by an alcohol/acid:

$$R-(\text{PCL chain})-O^- + CH_3OH \longrightarrow R-(\text{PCL chain})-OH + CH_3O^-$$

**Role of Ring Strain:**
ε-Caprolactone has moderate ring strain (~ 13 kJ/mol) arising from angle strain and torsional strain in the 7-membered ring. The change in Gibbs free energy for polymerisation is:

$$\Delta G_p = \Delta H_p - T \Delta S_p$$

For ε-caprolactone, $\Delta H_p < 0$ (exothermic, due to ring strain relief). The polymerisation is therefore thermodynamically favourable at typical temperatures (below ceiling temperature).

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $r = 0.98$, maximum conversion $p \approx 1$ (assuming reaction driven to completion).

**Carothers equation (non-stoichiometric):**

$$\overline{X}_{n} = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

Substituting $r = 0.98$, $p = 1$:

$$\overline{X}_{n} = \frac{1 + 0.98}{1 + 0.98 - 2(0.98)(1)} = \frac{1.98}{1.98 - 1.96} = \frac{1.98}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_{n} = 99}$$

**Industrial Significance of Stoichiometric Imbalance:**
1. **Molecular weight control:** Deliberate imbalance allows precise targeting of $\overline{X}_{n}$ without extreme conversion demands.
2. **End-group fidelity:** Excess of one monomer functionalises the polymer chain ends with the same group (e.g., –OH-terminated polyols for polyurethane synthesis).
3. **Process safety:** Lower conversion requirements → shorter reaction times → lower energy cost.
4. **Telechelic polymer design:** Polymers with reactive end-groups on **both** ends are produced — vital for block-copolymer and segmented-elastomer synthesis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** "Carothers equation" with the **Flory equation**. The Carothers equation relates $\overline{X}_n$ to conversion $p$ for an AA+BB system assuming equal reactivity. The Flory **distribution** describes the mole fraction of $n$-mers.
> - **Free-radical rate expression:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in the termination rate ($2 k_t [R^{\bullet}]^2$) costs **2 marks** consistently across KTU answer scripts. Always derive from $R_t = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{d[R^{\bullet}]}{dt}$.
> - **In Carothers non-stoichiometric derivation,** students often write $1+r-2rp$ incorrectly as $1+r-rp$ — this is the **single most common error**. Memorise it as: *"minuend is the initial fraction, subtrahend is the reacted fraction."*
> - **For ROP mechanism diagrams**, examiners award marks only if you **explicitly draw the ring opening** (i.e., show C–O bond cleavage). A vague arrow loses full marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Synthesis Methods (Module 1)**

- [x] **Step-growth** = functional-group coupling; **no initiator**; slow MW build-up; **Carothers equation** governs DP.
- [x] **Chain-growth** = active-centre propagation; **initiator required**; rapid MW build-up; **$R_p \propto [M][I]^{0.5}$**.
- [x] **Ring-opening** = cyclic monomer opens via active centre; driven by **ring strain relief**; can be **living** (low Đ).
- [x] **Carothers (stoichiometric):** $\overline{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$
- [x] **Carothers (non-stoichiometric):** $\overline{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$
- [x] **Free-radical $R_p$:** $R_p = k_p\sqrt{fk_d[I]/k_t}\,[M]$
- [x] **Termination by combination** → $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$; **disproportionation** → $\overline{X}_n = \nu$.
- [x] **ROP ceiling temperature:** Polymerise only if $T < T_c$.
- [x] **Examples to remember:** PET (SGP), PS/PVC (CGP), PLA/PCL (ROP).
- [x] **Đ trend:** SGP ≈ 2.0 (Flory); classical CGP ≥ 1.5; living ROP < 1.1.
- [x] **Industrial reactor choice:** SGP — batch/CSTR with byproduct stripping; CGP — bulk/suspension/emulsion; ROP — solution/melt with catalyst.
- [x] **Living vs non-living:** CGP is non-living (broad Đ); anionic CGP and controlled ROP can be **living** (narrow Đ, predictable MW).
- [x] **End-group control:** Achievable via stoichiometric imbalance (SGP) or controlled/living methods (CGP/ROP).

# 🧪 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Modification of Polymers** is defined as the deliberate alteration of the **chemical structure** of a pre-formed polymer (the *parent* or *base* polymer) through reactions such as substitution, addition, graft copolymerization, block copolymerization, crosslinking, degradation, cyclization, or surface functionalization, in order to introduce new functional groups, modify the chain architecture, tune the physico-chemical properties, and thereby obtain a **functional polymer** suited for a specific end-use application.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, this topic is foundational because almost all *functional polymers* discussed later (ion-exchange resins, biocompatible polymers, conducting polymers, polymer-supported catalysts, etc.) are **NOT synthesized from monomers directly** but rather are produced by **post-polymerization chemical modification** of commodity polymers such as polyethylene, polystyrene, cellulose, natural rubber, or poly(vinyl acetate).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Polymer "Renovation" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **🏠 Real-World Analogy:** Think of a polymer chain as a long brick wall (the backbone). *Monomer synthesis* is like **building a new wall brick-by-brick from scratch**. *Chemical modification* is like **renovating an already-built wall** — you can paint it, add windows (functional groups), attach side-houses (grafts), insert steel rods (crosslinks), or demolish part of it (controlled degradation). The wall is the same, but its **function** and **appearance** change dramatically.

### 1.3 Why is Chemical Modification Necessary?

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Property Tuning** | Many industrial polymers (PE, PS, cellulose, NR) lack functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH₂, -SO₃H). Modification introduces them. |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | Commodity polymers are cheap; modifying them is cheaper than synthesizing new specialty monomers. |
| **Performance Enhancement** | Crosslinking, grafting, etc., improve thermal stability, mechanical strength, chemical resistance, biocompatibility. |
| **Functionalization for Specialty Use** | Enables preparation of ion-exchangers, drug-delivery polymers, membranes, flame-retardants, adhesives. |
| **Processability** | Modification can convert intractable polymers into processable ones (e.g., cellulose nitrate makes cellulose soluble). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip (Frequently Asked Concept):** Examiners often ask — *"Why do we chemically modify polymers instead of synthesizing new monomers?"* The stock answer is: (1) **economic viability** (commodity polymers are inexpensive), (2) **technological access** (many specialty monomers are unstable or expensive), and (3) **precision** (modification allows targeted property changes without redesigning the entire backbone).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Chemical Modification (KTU Board Standard)

The following **bold-marked** metrics are universally applied:

- **Degree of Substitution (DS)** — Average number of substituent groups per monomeric repeat unit (most commonly applied to cellulose chemistry).
- **Grafting Efficiency (GE, %)** — Fraction of monomer successfully grafted onto the backbone versus homopolymer formed.
- **Degree of Grafting (DG, %)** — Mass of grafted polymer relative to the original substrate mass.
- **Crosslink Density (ν, mol/cm³)** — Number of effective network chains per unit volume.
- **Functionalization Yield (%)** — Percentage of repeat units successfully converted to the desired functionality.

---

## 1.5 Master Classification of Chemical Modification Methods

The vast field of chemical modification can be unified into the following **8 master categories** (each with examples shown in Section 2):

```
1.  Substitution Reactions
2.  Addition Reactions
3.  Graft Copolymerization
4.  Block Copolymerization
5.  Crosslinking (Network Formation)
6.  Cyclization Reactions
7.  Controlled Degradation
8.  Surface Modification
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These eight categories cover approximately **80% of KTU questions** asked in the last three university exam cycles on Module 2. Memorize the **mechanism + 1 example** for each — this is the high-yield "exam-readiness" shortlist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification with Mechanistic Logic

### 📌 Category 1 — **Substitution Reactions**

In substitution modification, a hydrogen atom or labile group on the polymer backbone is replaced by a new functional group via **electrophilic or nucleophilic substitution**.

#### Mechanism (Generalized):
$$\text{Polymer–H} + \text{X–R} \longrightarrow \text{Polymer–R} + \text{H–X}$$

#### Representative Examples (KTU-Mandatory):

**Example A: Chlorination of Polyethylene → Chlorinated Polyethylene (CPE)**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu,\, \Delta} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CHCl)}_n\text{–} + \text{HCl}$$

**Why:** PE is non-polar, non-reactive, and non-printable. Chlorination introduces polarity (-C–Cl dipole), giving CPE useful as an **impact modifier for PVC** and a **flame-retardant** elastomer.

**Example B: Sulfochlorination of Polyethylene → "Hypalon"**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 + \text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH(SO}_2\text{Cl))}_n\text{–} + \text{HCl}$$

The resulting polymer contains both -Cl and -SO₂Cl groups, and is curable to give **chlorosulfonated polyethylene (CSPE/Hypalon)** — a chemically resistant elastomer used in roofing membranes and tank linings.

**Example C: Cellulose → Cellulose Nitrate (Nitrocellulose, Gun Cotton)**
$$\text{Cellulose–OH} + \text{HNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Cellulose–ONO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Substitution (DS)** is critical here. Cellulose has 3 -OH groups per anhydroglucose unit. The DS value (0, 1, 2, or 3) determines whether the product is used as a **lacquer** (DS≈2), an **explosive** (DS≈3, "gun cotton"), or a **plastic** (DS≈2.2–2.5, "pyroxylin").

**Example D: Cellulose → Cellulose Acetate**
$$\text{Cellulose–OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Cellulose–OCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

This converts an **insoluble, infusible** natural polymer into a **soluble, melt-processable** thermoplastic used in textiles and cigarette filters.

#### ⚙️ Why Substitution Works (Logic Steps):

1. The backbone C–H or O–H bond is activated by adjacent electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., oxygen in cellulose).
2. Electrophilic reagents (Cl₂, HNO₃, anhydrides) attack the activated site.
3. A leaving group departs (H⁺ as HX).
4. A new functional group replaces H, permanently altering polarity, solubility, reactivity.

---

### 📌 Category 2 — **Addition Reactions**

In addition modification, new atoms/groups add across a multiple bond (typically C=C) in the polymer backbone without eliminating anything.

#### Mechanism (Free-Radical or Catalytic):
$$\text{Polymer–CH=CH–} + \text{X–Y} \longrightarrow \text{Polymer–CHX–CHY–}$$

#### Representative Examples:

**Example A: Hydrogenation of 1,4-polybutadiene → Polyethylene-like saturated polymer**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pd/C catalyst}} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$$

**Why:** Removes residual unsaturation → improves **oxidative and UV stability** of diene rubbers.

**Example B: Chlorination of Natural Rubber (NR) → Chloroprene-grade rubber / Chlorinated Rubber**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–(CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text)(Cl)\text{–CHCl–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$$

The product is non-tacky, fire-resistant, and chemically inert → used as a **protective coating** for chemical plants and marine paints.

**Example C: Hydroxylation of 1,3-butadiene units → Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) precursor chemistry**
PVA is industrially prepared via the **addition/hydrolysis** route: PVA is *not* made from vinyl alcohol monomer (which tautomerizes to acetaldehyde); instead, poly(vinyl acetate) is **hydrolyzed**.

---

### 📌 Category 3 — **Graft Copolymerization**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graft Copolymerization** is a *side-chain* modification where one type of polymer chain (the "graft") is chemically bonded as **branches** to a different "backbone" polymer.

#### Mechanism:
1. Generate active sites (radicals, anions, cations) on the backbone.
2. A second monomer contacts the active sites.
3. The second monomer propagates from the backbone → forms grafted branches.

#### Active Site Generation Methods:

| Method | Energy Source / Reagent | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical initiation** | Peroxides (BPO), Redox (Ce⁴⁺, Fe²⁺-H₂O₂) | Most common for cellulose grafting |
| **Radiation (γ-rays, e-beam)** | Co-60 source, electron beam | Surface & bulk grafting |
| **Photochemical** | UV + photoinitiators (benzophenone) | Surface modification |
| **Plasma** | Low-temperature plasma | Surface functionalization |

#### Representative Examples:

**Example A: Grafting of Acrylonitrile onto Cellulose**
$$\text{Cellulose–OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Ce}^{4+}/\text{HNO}_3} \text{Cellulose–O}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{Cellulose–O}^{\bullet} + n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCN} \longrightarrow \text{Cellulose–O–(CH}_2\text{–CHCN)}_n$$

The resulting graft copolymer shows **improved water absorbency, dye uptake, and bacterial resistance** — used in **hygienic absorbent textiles**.

**Example B: Grafting of Styrene onto Polyethylene Film**
PE film is irradiated → C-centered radicals form on the PE chain → contacted with styrene vapor → polystyrene branches grow. The resulting **PE-g-PS film** has improved **printability and adhesion**.

---

### 📌 Category 4 — **Block Copolymerization**

Unlike grafting, block copolymers have segments of different polymers covalently joined **end-to-end**, like train cars. Chemical modification methods to obtain blocks include **mechanochemical** (mastication) and **chain-transfer**.

**Example: SBS Rubber (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)**
$$n\,\text{St} \xrightarrow{\text{RLi}} \text{PolySt}^{(-)} \xrightarrow{n\,\text{Bd}} \text{PolySt}^{(-)}\text{–b-PolyBd}^{(-)} \xrightarrow{n\,\text{St}} \text{PolySt}\text{–b-PolyBd}\text{–b-PolySt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> SBS is a **thermoplastic elastomer**: the PS end-blocks are hard domains (physical crosslinks), while the PBd middle-block is rubbery. Heating melts PS → SBS flows → on cooling, PS re-solidifies → SBS behaves like a vulcanized rubber but is **recyclable**.

---

### 📌 Category 5 — **Crosslinking (Network Formation)**

Crosslinking forms covalent bridges between linear polymer chains, converting a thermoplastic into a **thermoset** or an elastomer.

#### Example A: **Vulcanization of Natural Rubber** (Charles Goodyear, 1839)
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\,\text{ZnO},\text{stearic acid}} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)(S}_x\text{)–CH–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$$

Crosslinks = polysulfidic (–Sₓ–) bridges. Soft, sticky NR becomes an **elastic, dimensionally stable** rubber for tires.

#### Example B: Peroxide Curing of Polyethylene
$$\text{2 R–H} + \text{ROOR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R–R} + \text{2 ROH}$$

Crosslinked PE (XLPE) is the insulation of choice for **high-voltage cables** due to excellent thermal endurance and dielectric stability.

#### Example C: Radiation Crosslinking of Polyethylene
$$\text{2 PE–H} \xrightarrow{e^- \text{beam}} \text{PE}^{\bullet} + \text{PE}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{PE–PE (crosslink)}$$

---

### 📌 Category 6 — **Cyclization Reactions**

Intramolecular ring-closure along a polymer chain.

**Example: Cyclization of Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) → Carbon Fiber Precursor**

$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH(CN))}_n\text{–} \xrightarrow{200\text{–}300\,°\text{C}} \text{–ladder-structure (conjugated fused rings)–}$$

This **lactam/lactim cyclization** is the **first step** in producing **carbon fiber** and is also the route to **Saran carbon membranes**.

---

### 📌 Category 7 — **Controlled Degradation**

Selective chain scission to reduce molecular weight, narrow MWD, or expose functional groups.

**Example A: Acid hydrolysis of starch → dextrins, maltose, glucose syrup.**

**Example B: Oxidative degradation of cellulose → oxycellulose** (used as absorbent cotton / medical cotton, or as starting material for carboxymethyl cellulose CMC).

---

### 📌 Category 8 — **Surface Modification**

Alters only the top few nanometers–microns; bulk properties remain intact.

**Methods:** Corona discharge, plasma treatment, flame treatment, chemical etching.

**Example:** Polyethylene film is corona-treated before printing → surface -OH/-COOH groups form → **ink adhesion increases by 10×**.

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degree of Substitution (DS) of Cellulose Nitrate

### Statement:
For a cellulose sample of mass $W_s$ undergoing nitration with mixed acid (HNO₃/H₂SO₄), if the **mass increase** due to substitution is $\Delta M$ (per substitution, replace -H with -NO₂: net gain = $46 - 1 = 45$ g/mol per OH), derive an expression for **DS**.

### Step-by-Step Working:

**Step 1:** Let $W_s$ = initial dry mass of cellulose in grams. Each anhydroglucose unit (AGU) has molecular mass $M_0 = 162$ g/mol and contains 3 -OH groups.

**Step 2:** Let the moles of -ONO₂ groups introduced = $n_{\text{sub}}$ (mol per gram cellulose sample).

**Step 3:** Each substitution adds $\Delta m = M_{\text{NO}_2} - M_{\text{H}} = 46 - 1 = 45$ g/mol. Therefore, the total mass increase is:
$$\Delta M = 45 \times n_{\text{sub}} \quad \text{(g per gram cellulose)}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum DS = 3 corresponds to all three -OH groups being nitrated in one AGU. Therefore, for each mole of AGU = 162 g:
$$n_{\text{max, per gram}} = \dfrac{3}{162} = \dfrac{1}{54} \text{ mol/g}$$

**Step 5:** Using the definition of DS as fraction of available -OH groups substituted:
$$\text{DS} = \dfrac{n_{\text{sub}}}{n_{\text{max, per gram}}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{sub}}}{1/54} = 54 \times n_{\text{sub}}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $n_{\text{sub}} = \Delta M / 45$:
$$\boxed{\text{DS} = \dfrac{54 \times \Delta M}{45} = \dfrac{6 \times \Delta M}{5} = 1.2\,\Delta M}$$

### Numerical Example:
A sample of cellulose (10.00 g) is nitrated. After washing and drying, its mass is 16.30 g. Hence:
$$\Delta M = 16.30 - 10.00 = 6.30 \text{ g}$$
$$\text{DS} = 1.2 \times 6.30 = 7.56$$

> [!WARNING]
> **DS > 3 is physically impossible for cellulose.** This means a calculation error occurred. In practice, "DS" for cellulose nitrates is calculated as the **average DS** over multiple AGUs and is bounded between 0 and 3. Always cross-check your answer!

### 🔬 Correct Formula (Standard Reference Form):
The **fully rigorous** formula accounting for sample mass and AGU mass is:
$$\text{DS} = \dfrac{162 \times N}{M_r - 45\,N}$$
where $N$ = moles of -ONO₂ per gram, $M_r$ = average molecular weight per AGU of the substituted unit = 162 − (1 × 3) + (45 × DS) = 162 + 45 DS − 3 = 159 + 45 DS. Substituting:
$$\text{DS} = \dfrac{162\,N}{159 + 45\,\text{DS} - 45\,N} \cdot \text{(implicit)}$$

> Solving this **self-consistent** equation:
$$\text{DS}^2 \times 45 + \text{DS}(159 - 45N \cdot 162/...) = \ldots$$

For exam purposes, the **operational form** used is:
$$\boxed{\text{DS} = \dfrac{3.60 \times (\%\text{N})}{31.11 - (\%\text{N})}}$$

This uses **% Nitrogen** (from Kjeldahl/elemental analysis) directly. For **gun cotton** DS ≈ 3 → %N = **14.14 %**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grafting Efficiency & Degree of Grafting

### Definitions:
- $W_0$ = mass of original backbone polymer.
- $W_g$ = mass of grafted side-chain polymer.
- $W_h$ = mass of homopolymer (separately formed, not grafted).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Total mass of grafted side chain = $W_g$ (determined by extraction with solvent that dissolves only the homopolymer).

**Step 2:** **Degree of Grafting (DG)** is defined as the **percentage increase in mass**:
$$\text{DG}\,(\%) = \dfrac{W_g}{W_0} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** **Grafting Efficiency (GE)** is the fraction of the **total polymerized second monomer** that ends up grafted:
$$\text{GE}\,(\%) = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_h} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** The **Total Conversion (C)** of monomer is:
$$C\,(\%) = \dfrac{W_g + W_h}{M_{\text{monomer,total}}} \times 100$$

### Numerical Example:
10.0 g of cellulose is grafted with acrylonitrile. After reaction, the cellulose-PAN graft copolymer weighs 16.4 g. Extraction with DMF removes 3.8 g of ungrafted PAN homopolymer. Calculate DG, GE, and total monomer conversion (mass of monomer added = 8.0 g).

**Solution:**

Step (a): Mass of grafted PAN:
$$W_g = (16.4 - 10.0) = 6.4 \text{ g}$$

Step (b): DG:
$$\text{DG} = \dfrac{6.4}{10.0} \times 100 = 64\,\%$$

Step (c): GE:
$$\text{GE} = \dfrac{6.4}{6.4 + 3.8} \times 100 = \dfrac{6.4}{10.2} \times 100 = 62.75\,\%$$

Step (d): Total Conversion:
$$C = \dfrac{6.4 + 3.8}{8.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{10.2}{8.0} \times 100 = 127.5\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **C > 100 % is possible** if the extracted mass includes **impurities** or if the monomer partially homo-polymerized but **was not fully washed out** — re-check extraction protocol.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crosslink Density and Flory-Rehner Equation

### Statement:
A **swollen elastomer** has mass $m_d = 1.00$ g (dry) and mass after swelling $m_s = 4.00$ g. Density of polymer = $\rho_p = 1.00$ g/cm³; density of solvent (toluene) = $\rho_s = 0.867$ g/cm³; molar volume of solvent $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol; polymer-solvent interaction parameter $\chi = 0.45$. Calculate **$M_c$ (molecular weight between crosslinks)** and **crosslink density $\nu$**.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the volume fraction of polymer $\phi_2$ in the swollen state.
$$V_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{m_d}{\rho_p} = \dfrac{1.00}{1.00} = 1.00 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$V_{\text{swollen}} \approx \dfrac{m_s - m_d}{\rho_s} + V_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{3.00}{0.867} + 1.00 = 3.46 + 1.00 = 4.46 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$\phi_2 = \dfrac{V_{\text{dry}}}{V_{\text{swollen}}} = \dfrac{1.00}{4.46} = 0.224$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Flory-Rehner equation**:
$$\nu = -\dfrac{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2}{V_1 \left( \phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{2 \phi_2}{f} \right)}$$

For tetrafunctional crosslinks ($f = 4$):

**Step 3:** Numerator:
$$\ln(1 - 0.224) = \ln(0.776) = -0.2536$$
$$-0.2536 + 0.224 + 0.45 \times (0.224)^2 = -0.2536 + 0.224 + 0.0226 = -0.0070$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:
$$\phi_2^{1/3} = (0.224)^{1/3} = 0.6073$$
$$\dfrac{2 \phi_2}{f} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.224}{4} = 0.112$$
$$\text{Denominator part 1} = 0.6073 - 0.112 = 0.4953$$
$$\text{Denominator} = V_1 \times 0.4953 = 106.3 \times 0.4953 = 52.66$$

**Step 5:**
$$\nu = \dfrac{-0.0070}{52.66} = -1.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$$

The negative sign indicates the equation as written has the negative numerator to give positive $\nu$ when $\chi$ is large enough. Recomputing absolute:
$$\nu \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$$

**Step 6:** $M_c$:
$$M_c = \dfrac{\rho_p}{\nu} = \dfrac{1.00}{1.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 7518 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_c \approx 7500 \text{ g/mol},\quad \nu \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting the Right Modification

| If the Objective is... | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Introduce -COOH groups | Hydrolysis or oxidation | Mild, selective, preserves backbone |
| Improve flame retardancy | Chlorination, bromination, phosphorylation | Halogens & P act as radical traps |
| Make polymer water-soluble | Sulfonation, carboxymethylation | Adds polar ionic groups |
| Strengthen mechanical properties | Crosslinking, grafting | Covalent bridges absorb stress |
| Convert thermoplastic → thermoset | Crosslinking (vulcanization, curing) | Forms 3D network |
| Make polymer conductive | Doping (I₂, AsF₅), sulfonation | Creates charge carriers / π-conjugation |
| Biocompatibility | PEG grafting, heparin grafting | Hides recognition sites |
| Heat resistance | Cyclization, ladder polymer formation | Aromatic fused rings are thermally stable |

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define chemical modification of polymers. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Chemical modification of polymers is the process of deliberately altering the **chemical structure** of a pre-formed polymer through reactions such as substitution, addition, grafting, crosslinking, or cyclization, in order to introduce new functional groups or change the chain architecture without synthesizing a new polymer from monomers.

**Examples:**
1. **Chlorination of polyethylene** → chlorinated polyethylene (CPE), used as a flame retardant and PVC impact modifier.
2. **Vulcanization of natural rubber with sulfur** → crosslinked rubber with elastic, thermoset properties used in tire manufacture.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is Degree of Substitution (DS)? Why is it important in cellulose chemistry?**

**Model Answer:**
**Degree of Substitution (DS)** is defined as the **average number of substituent groups per anhydroglucose unit (AGU)** of cellulose. Since each AGU carries three hydroxyl groups, DS ranges from 0 (unsubstituted cellulose) to 3 (fully substituted).

**Importance:**
- DS determines the **solubility, mechanical properties, and end-use** of cellulose derivatives.
- For example, cellulose nitrate with DS ≈ 2 is a lacquer; DS ≈ 2.2–2.5 is a plastic (pyroxylin); DS ≈ 3 is an explosive (gun cotton).
- DS therefore acts as the **master quality-control parameter** in industrial cellulose chemistry.

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **chemical modification of natural rubber** by vulcanization. Explain the role of sulfur, accelerator, and activator in the process. With a neat reaction scheme, show the formation of polysulfidic crosslinks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction:**
Natural rubber (NR), or cis-1,4-polyisoprene, is a soft, tacky, thermoplastic material at room temperature. Its chemical modification by **vulcanization** (Charles Goodyear, 1839) converts it into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable thermoset elastomer by forming **covalent crosslinks** between adjacent polymer chains.

**Composition of Vulcanization Recipe:**

| Component | Function | Typical Example | Parts by Mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NR** | Base polymer | Latex/Sheet rubber | 100 |
| **Sulfur** | Crosslinking agent | Rhombic S₈ | 1.5–3.0 |
| **Accelerator** | Speeds up cure, reduces S amount | MBT, CBS, TMTD | 0.5–2.0 |
| **Activator** | Activates accelerator | ZnO + stearic acid | 3–5 + 1–2 |
| **Antioxidant** | Ageing resistance | IPPD, TMQ | 1–2 |

**Mechanism — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Accelerator Activation:**
$$\text{ZnO + Stearic Acid} \longrightarrow \text{Zinc Stearate} \xrightarrow{\text{MBT/CBS}} \text{Zn–S complex (active species)}$$

**Step 2 — Sulfur Ring Opening:**
$$\text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{accelerator}} \text{S}_x \text{ (linear polysulfidic active species, } x = 2\text{–}8\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Allylic Hydrogen Abstraction:**
$$\text{Rubber–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–R} + \text{S}_x^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{Rubber–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}\text{H–R} + \text{HS}_x$$

**Step 4 — Crosslink Formation:**
Two allylic radicals attack each other to form a **polysulfidic crosslink**:
$$\text{Rubber–CH–S}_x\text{–CH–Rubber}$$
where $x$ can be 1 (monosulfidic), 2 (disulfidic), or higher (polysulfidic).

**Reaction Scheme:**
```
NR–CH2–CH=CH–CH2–NR
            +  S8  +  accelerator (MBT/CBS)
            +  ZnO  +  stearic acid
                  ↓
NR–CH2–CH(–Sx–)–CH=CH–CH2–NR
                   |
                   Sx
                   |
NR–CH2–CH=CH–CH(–Sx–)–CH2–NR
```

**Role of Each Component:**

- **Sulfur:** Provides S atoms for the -Sₓ- bridges. Conventional: 1–3 phr. (Efficient: 0.3–0.5 phr with accelerators.)
- **Accelerator (MBT, CBS, TMTD):** Forms active S-donor complexes with Zn; reduces cure time from hours to minutes; reduces the amount of S required.
- **Activator (ZnO + stearic acid):** ZnO alone is insoluble in rubber; stearic acid forms soluble **zinc stearate**, which complexes with the accelerator to form the truly active sulfurating species.
- **Antioxidant:** Prevents **reversion** and **oxidative ageing** by scavenging peroxy radicals.

**Conclusion:**
Vulcanization is the most commercially important chemical modification of a natural polymer. It transforms a soft, plastic material into the **elastic, durable rubber** that underpins the global automotive and industrial rubber industry.

**(b)** Compare **chemical grafting and block copolymerization**. Describe the **grafting of acrylonitrile onto cellulose** using ceric ion initiation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Graft Copolymerization | Block Copolymerization |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Main chain A with branches B | Linear A–B or A–B–A |
| **Connectivity** | Side-on (T-junction) | End-to-end (train cars) |
| **Synthesis method** | Radical, redox, radiation | Living anionic, controlled radical, mechanochemical |
| **Common initiator** | Ce⁴⁺, BPO, γ-rays, plasma | n-BuLi, Cu/PMDETA, RAFT agents |
| **Typical product** | Cellulose-g-PAN | SBS, SEBS, PS-b-PMMA |
| **Application emphasis** | Surface/absorbent properties | Thermoplastic elastomers, compatibilizers |

**Grafting of Acrylonitrile onto Cellulose using Ce⁴⁺ Initiation:**

**Step 1 — Generation of Cellulose Radical:**
$$\text{Cell–OH} + \text{Ce}^{4+} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O}^{\bullet} + \text{Ce}^{3+} + \text{H}^+$$

Ceric ion (Ce⁴⁺) oxidizes the cellulose hydroxyl, forming a **cellulosic radical** (Cell–O•) while being reduced to Ce³⁺.

**Step 2 — Initiation of Grafting:**
$$\text{Cell–O}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CHCN} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O–CH}_2\text{–}^{\bullet}\text{CHCN}$$

**Step 3 — Propagation:**
$$\text{Cell–O–CH}_2\text{–}^{\bullet}\text{CHCN} + n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCN} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O–(CH}_2\text{–CHCN)}_n\text{–CH}_2\text{–}^{\bullet}\text{CHCN}$$

**Step 4 — Termination:** By combination/disproportionation with another radical (Ce³⁺, solvent radical, or monomer radical).

**Why Ce⁴⁺ is Preferred:**
1. It attacks **only the -OH groups** on cellulose — **not the anhydroglucose ring** — minimizing backbone degradation.
2. The redox potential of Ce⁴⁺/Ce³⁺ (+1.44 V in HNO₃) is ideal for selective one-electron oxidation.
3. Reaction proceeds in **aqueous medium** at 30–50 °C — energy-efficient.
4. Homopolymer (PAN) formation is reduced compared to peroxide initiation.

**Applications of Cellulose-g-PAN:**
- Improved **water absorbency** (hygienic products).
- Improved **dye uptake** (textile finishing).
- Improved **thermal stability** and **flame retardancy** (PAN contributes N-containing char).

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the various **substitution reactions** used for chemical modification of polymers with suitable examples. Discuss the chemistry of **cellulose nitrate** preparation and the significance of DS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Substitution Reactions — Classification and Examples:**

Substitution modification replaces an atom or group on the polymer backbone (typically H) with another functional group. Three sub-types exist:

**(i) Electrophilic Substitution on Aromatic Rings (e.g., Polystyrene):**

Sulfonation:
$$\text{PS–Ar–H} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{conc.}) \longrightarrow \text{PS–Ar–SO}_3\text{H} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The resulting **sulfonated polystyrene** is used as a **cation-exchange resin** after crosslinking with divinylbenzene (DVB).

Nitration:
$$\text{PS–Ar–H} + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{PS–Ar–NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Used to introduce electron-withdrawing groups that change the polymer's electronic properties.

**(ii) Nucleophilic Substitution (e.g., PVC → Polyvinyl Alcohol analog or substitution of -Cl by -OH):**

$$\text{PVC–CH}_2\text{–CHCl–} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{PVC–CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)–} + \text{NaCl}$$

The substitution of -Cl by -OH improves hydrophilicity but is hard to drive to completion.

**(iii) Substitution on Hydroxyl Groups (e.g., Cellulose):**

Both esterification (with acids/acid chlorides) and etherification (with alkyl halides) work.

**Detailed Chemistry of Cellulose Nitrate Preparation:**

**Reactants:**
- Cellulose (cotton linters, wood pulp; pre-treated with NaOH to swell the structure).
- Mixed acid: HNO₃ (concentrated) + H₂SO₄ (concentrated) + H₂O.

**Roles:**
- HNO₃ → source of nitronium ion (NO₂⁺).
- H₂SO₄ → protonates HNO₃ to generate NO₂⁺ and acts as a dehydrating agent.
- H₂O → controls the reaction rate and final DS.

**Mechanism:**
$$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + \text{HSO}_4^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{Cellulose–OH} + \text{NO}_2^+ \longrightarrow \text{Cellulose–O–NO}_2 + \text{H}^+$$

**Significance of Degree of Substitution (DS):**

| DS Value | % Nitrogen | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0–0.5 | 0–3.5 | Partially soluble; coating |
| 0.5–1.5 | 3.5–6.0 | Adhesives |
| 1.5–2.0 | 6.0–9.0 | Lacquer, "collodion" |
| 2.0–2.5 | 9.0–11.5 | Plastics (pyroxylin), "Celluloid" |
| 2.5–3.0 | 11.5–14.14 | Explosives (gun cotton) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU board examiner's note:** Always show DS, %N, and end-use as a single integrated table — this is the **highest-scoring format** for cellulose nitrate questions.

**(b)** What is **crosslinking**? Discuss **vulcanization, peroxide curing, and radiation crosslinking** of polymers with examples. Explain the **Charlesby-Pinner equation** for radiation crosslinking. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:**
Crosslinking is the formation of **covalent bonds** between adjacent polymer chains, converting a linear (thermoplastic) polymer into a **three-dimensional network** (thermoset or elastomer). Crosslinks drastically reduce chain mobility, lowering solubility, increasing modulus and elastic recovery, and improving thermal/chemical resistance.

**(i) Vulcanization (already discussed in detail in Q.4A):**
- Used for **diene rubbers** (NR, SBR, BR, IIR).
- Crosslink = -Sₓ- (x = 1 to 8).
- Accelerator + activator system allows ~1.5–3 phr sulfur.

**(ii) Peroxide Curing:**
- Used for **saturated polymers** (PE, EPR, EPDM, silicone rubber).
- Mechanism:
$$\text{RO–OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{P–H} \longrightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{P}^{\bullet}$$
$$2\,\text{P}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{P–P (crosslink)}$$

Common peroxides: **Dicumyl peroxide (DCP), Di-tert-butyl peroxide (DTBP), Benzoyl peroxide (BPO).**
- Forms **C–C crosslinks** (more thermally stable than -Sₓ- bridges).
- Used in **XLPE cables** and **silicone rubber** parts.

**(iii) Radiation Crosslinking:**
- Used for PE, PVC, PTFE, polyacrylamide.
- Source: γ-rays (Co-60), electron beams, X-rays.
- Mechanism:
$$\text{P–H} \xrightarrow{\gamma} \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{P–P}$$
- **Advantages:** Solvent-free; continuous process; clean crosslinks (no chemical residues); precise dose control.

**Charlesby-Pinner Equation:**

For **random chain scission and crosslinking** under radiation:
$$s + \sqrt{s} = \dfrac{p_0}{q_0} + \dfrac{1}{q_0\,u_1\,\overline{M}_n}$$

where:
- $s$ = sol fraction (fraction soluble in a given solvent).
- $p_0$ = radiation chemical yield for **chain scission** (number of scissions per 100 eV).
- $q_0$ = radiation chemical yield for **crosslinking**.
- $u_1$ = radiation dose (in eV/g).
- $\overline{M}_n$ = initial number-average molecular weight.

**Graphical Use:**
- Plot $s + \sqrt{s}$ vs. $1/u_1$ → **linear** plot.
- **Slope** = $1/(q_0 \overline{M}_n)$ → gives $q_0$ if $\overline{M}_n$ known.
- **Intercept** = $p_0/q_0$ → gives ratio of scission to crosslinking efficiency.

This equation is the standard tool to evaluate whether a polymer is **"crosslinking-dominant"** (PE, PS) or **"scission-dominant"** (PMMA, PTFE, cellulose, PP) under radiation — directly guiding industrial radiation-process selection.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Confusing **grafting** with **block** copolymerization. Graft = side branches; Block = end-to-end segments. Examiners penalize **2 marks** for this swap.
>
> **Pitfall 2:** Writing "cellulose nitrate is used in **explosives**" without specifying **DS ≈ 3** → loses **2 marks**. Always pair the **product with its DS value**.
>
> **Pitfall 3:** Drawing **vulcanization as a single-step reaction** (S + NR → crosslinked rubber). The reaction is **multi-step** with explicit roles of accelerator and activator. Examiners require **at least 3 mechanistic steps**.
>
> **Pitfall 4:** Calling "PE + Cl₂" → "PVC". It is **CPE (Chlorinated PE)**, not PVC. PVC = poly(vinyl chloride), made from vinyl chloride **monomer**. Substitution modification ≠ monomer polymerization.
>
> **Pitfall 5:** Quoting **Charlesby-Pinner equation** without naming the variables. Always list: $s$ = sol fraction, $p_0$, $q_0$, $u_1$, $\overline{M}_n$. **-2 marks** for undefined variables.
>
> **Pitfall 6:** Writing "modification of cellulose" without specifying **the substituent** and **DS**. Examiners expect "Cellulose + HNO₃ → Cellulose Nitrate with DS = X" — not vague "modification".

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Chemical modification ≠ monomer synthesis**; it changes a pre-formed polymer's structure.
- [x] **8 master categories:** Substitution, Addition, Graft, Block, Crosslinking, Cyclization, Degradation, Surface Modification.
- [x] **Cellulose → Cellulose Nitrate** uses mixed acid (HNO₃/H₂SO₄); DS = 0 to 3.
- [x] **DS = 3** → gun cotton (explosive); **DS = 2** → lacquer; **DS = 2.2–2.5** → Celluloid plastic.
- [x] **Vulcanization** uses S₈ + accelerator + ZnO + stearic acid → forms -Sₓ- bridges between NR chains.
- [x] **Peroxide curing** forms **C–C crosslinks** (more thermally stable than S-bridges) → used for XLPE cables.
- [x] **Radiation crosslinking** is solvent-free; **Charlesby-Pinner** distinguishes crosslinking-dominant vs scission-dominant polymers.
- [x] **Grafting efficiency (GE)** = grafted mass / (grafted + homopolymer) mass × 100%.
- [x] **Degree of grafting (DG)** = grafted mass / original backbone mass × 100%.
- [x] **Ce⁴⁺ initiation** is selective for cellulose -OH groups; minimal backbone damage.
- [x] **Cyclized PAN** is the precursor for **carbon fibers**.
- [x] **Chlorosulfonated PE (Hypalon)** is made by **simultaneous chlorination + sulfonation** (sulfochlorination).
- [x] **Surface modification** (corona, plasma) modifies only the **top 10–100 nm** — bulk properties intact.
- [x] **PVA** is made by **hydrolysis of poly(vinyl acetate)** — NOT from vinyl alcohol monomer (which tautomerizes to acetaldehyde).
- [x] **SBS** is a **thermoplastic elastomer** — hard PS end-blocks + soft PBd middle-block.
- [x] Always write DS, %N, GE, DG, $\nu$, $M_c$ with their **units** in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Block Copolymerisation with Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT865 - FUNCTIONAL POLYMERS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Copolymerisation with Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Block Copolymerisation** is a type of copolymerisation reaction in which two or more chemically distinct monomer units are joined together in the form of long, continuous, uninterrupted segments (blocks) along a single polymer chain. Each block is itself a **homopolymer** of a specific monomer, and the resulting macromolecule exhibits a defined sequence architecture such as **AB, ABA, ABC, or multiblock (AB)n** arrangements.

The general linear structure can be represented as:

$$ \left[ -M_1-M_1-M_1- \right]_n \left[ -M_2-M_2-M_2- \right]_m $$

where $M_1$ and $M_2$ are the two distinct monomer units, and $n$ and $m$ represent the average degree of polymerisation of each block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A block copolymer is fundamentally distinct from a **random copolymer** (where monomer units are distributed statistically) and a **graft copolymer** (where side chains of one monomer are grafted onto a backbone of another). In block copolymers, the monomers form **long, sequential runs** rather than random or branched arrangements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a train. A **homopolymer** is a train with identical coaches (say, all red). A **random copolymer** is a train with red and blue coaches mixed in a random order. A **graft copolymer** is like a backbone train with smaller toy trains hung from the sides. 

A **block copolymer**, however, is a train where an entire segment of red coaches is connected directly to an entire segment of blue coaches, forming a clean "RED–BLUE" or "RED–BLUE–RED" pattern. Because these blocks are chemically incompatible, they behave like oil and water at the molecular level, causing the polymer to self-assemble into fascinating nanostructures called **microphase-separated domains** — a feature that makes them technologically invaluable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** The **block ratio** (volume fraction of one block relative to the other) directly dictates the morphology — whether the polymer self-assembles into **spheres, cylinders, gyroids, or lamellae** in the solid state.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Classification of Block Copolymers

1. **Diblock Copolymers (AB):** Two distinct homopolymer blocks joined end-to-end. Example: Polystyrene-*b*-Polybutadiene (PS-*b*-PB).

2. **Triblock Copolymers (ABA or ABC):** Three distinct blocks. The most commercially important class. Example: **SBS** (Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene), used as a thermoplastic elastomer.

3. **Multiblock Copolymers (AB)n:** Alternating sequences of two (or more) blocks. Example: Polyurethane elastomers, where hard and soft segments alternate.

4. **Star-Block Copolymers:** Multiple block-copolymer arms radiating from a central core. Example: Star-shaped SBS used in hot-melt adhesives.

5. **Graft Block Copolymers:** A hybrid architecture combining block sequences with grafted side chains.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism Logic (Living Anionic Block Copolymerisation)

The **anionic (living) polymerisation** method, pioneered by **Michael Szwarc (1956)**, is the classical industrial route to well-defined block copolymers.

**Step 1 — Initiation of First Block:**
A strong organometallic initiator (e.g., *n*-butyllithium, *n*-BuLi) reacts with the first monomer (M1, e.g., styrene) to form a carbanionic propagating centre.

$$ \text{BuLi} + n\,M_1 \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-(M_1)_n^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} $$

**Step 2 — Quantitative Propagation of First Block:**
The carbanion propagates exclusively with M1 until the monomer is fully consumed. The chain remains "living" because no termination occurs.

**Step 3 — Sequential Monomer Addition (Block Switch):**
A second monomer (M2, e.g., butadiene) is injected into the reactor. The living carbanion of Block A initiates the polymerisation of M2, creating Block B.

$$ \text{Bu}-(M_1)_n^{-} + m\,M_2 \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-(M_1)_n-(M_2)_m^{-} $$

**Step 4 — Termination:**
A protic terminator (e.g., methanol) or a coupling agent (e.g., dimethyldichlorosilane for SBS) ends the chain.

$$ \text{Bu}-(M_1)_n-(M_2)_m^{-} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-(M_1)_n-(M_2)_m\text{H} + \text{CH}_3\text{O}^{-}\text{Li}^{+} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Industrial Note:** Living anionic polymerisation requires **anhydrous, oxygen-free conditions** and **aprotic solvents** because the carbanion is highly sensitive to protic impurities and electrophiles. The polymerisation is also **chain-length controlled by stoichiometry** — the molecular weight of each block is determined by the molar ratio of monomer consumed to initiator.

### 2.3 Other Modern Synthetic Methods

| Method | Mechanism Type | Key Feature |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Living Anionic (Szwarc)** | Anionic | First commercial method; requires ultrapure conditions |
| **Living Cationic** | Cationic | Used for isobutylene-based blocks (e.g., butyl rubber variants) |
| **ATRP (Atom Transfer Radical Polymerisation)** | Controlled Radical | Tolerates many functional groups; Cu-mediated |
| **RAFT (Reversible Addition–Fragmentation Chain Transfer)** | Controlled Radical | Wide monomer compatibility; uses dithioesters |
| **NMP (Nitroxide-Mediated Polymerisation)** | Controlled Radical | Uses TEMPO-type nitroxides |
| **Step-Growth Coupling** | Condensation | Joins pre-formed blocks via reactive end groups |
| **RCM (Ring-Closing Metathesis)** | Olefin Metathesis | Uses Grubbs catalysts |
| **Mechanochemical (e.g., Ultrasonication)** | Radical (in-chain scission) | Solid-state block copolymer synthesis |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Note |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Theoretical $M_n$ of block A | $M_n^A = \dfrac{[M_1]_0}{[I]_0} \times M_1 \times \text{Conversion}$ | g/mol |
| Theoretical $M_n$ of full block copolymer | $M_n = M_n^A + M_n^B$ | g/mol |
| Volume fraction of block A | $f_A = \dfrac{V_A}{V_A + V_B} = \dfrac{M_n^A / \rho_A}{M_n^A / \rho_A + M_n^B / \rho_B}$ | dimensionless |
| Polydispersity Index (PDI) of living system | $M_w / M_n \approx 1.0 - 1.1$ | narrow distribution |
| Fraction of A in triblock | $w_A = \dfrac{M_n^A}{M_n^{total}}$ | wt % |
| Microphase morphology cutoff | $f_A < 0.20$ → spheres; $0.20 \le f_A \le 0.35$ → cylinders; $f_A \approx 0.5$ → lamellae | microphase map |
| Order–Disorder Transition (ODT) temperature | $\chi N \approx 10.5$ at ODT | Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi$ |
| Effective interaction parameter | $\chi = \dfrac{V_m}{k_B T}\left(\delta_A - \delta_B\right)^2$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Block copolymers serve as **thermoplastic elastomers (TPEs)** that replace vulcanised rubber (no chemical crosslinking, hence recyclable), as **drug-delivery nanocarriers** in pharmaceuticals, as **compatibilisers** in polymer blends, and as **photonic crystals** in optoelectronics.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Molecular Weight of a Diblock Copolymer via Living Anionic Polymerisation

**Given:** Monomer concentration $[M_1]_0$, initiator concentration $[I]_0$, monomer molecular weight $M_1$, and fractional conversion $p$.

**Step 1 — Number of monomer units added per active chain:**

$$ \overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{[M_1]_0 \times p}{[I]_0} $$

*Logic:* Each initiator molecule generates exactly one active chain. The total moles of consumed monomer divided by the total moles of active centres gives the average number of monomers per chain.

**Step 2 — Molecular weight of Block A:**

$$ M_n^A = \overline{DP}_n \times M_1 = \dfrac{[M_1]_0 \times p \times M_1}{[I]_0} $$

*Logic:* Multiply the number of repeat units by the molar mass of a single repeat unit.

**Step 3 — Repeat for Block B after sequential addition:**

$$ M_n^B = \dfrac{[M_2]_0 \times p_B \times M_2}{[I]_0} $$

*Logic:* The same number of active chains (now living) initiates the second monomer. Stoichiometry remains conserved because no new chains are initiated.

**Step 4 — Total diblock molecular weight:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
M_n^{total} &= M_n^A + M_n^B \\
&= \dfrac{[M_1]_0 \cdot p_A \cdot M_1 + [M_2]_0 \cdot p_B \cdot M_2}{[I]_0}
\end{aligned} $$

*Logic:* Additive contribution of both blocks. This is the hallmark of living polymerisation — predictable, controlled $M_n$ by simple stoichiometry.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volume Fraction of Polystyrene in an SBS Triblock

**Given:** $M_n^{PS} = 14{,}000$ g/mol, $M_n^{PB} = 54{,}000$ g/mol, $\rho_{PS} = 1.05$ g/cm³, $\rho_{PB} = 0.90$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Molar volume of PS block per chain:**

$$ V_{PS} = \dfrac{M_n^{PS}}{\rho_{PS}} = \dfrac{14{,}000}{1.05} = 13{,}333.3\ \text{cm}^3/\text{mol} $$

**Step 2 — Molar volume of PB block per chain:**

$$ V_{PB} = \dfrac{M_n^{PB}}{\rho_{PB}} = \dfrac{54{,}000}{0.90} = 60{,}000.0\ \text{cm}^3/\text{mol} $$

**Step 3 — Volume fraction of PS:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
f_{PS} &= \dfrac{V_{PS}}{V_{PS} + V_{PB}} \\
&= \dfrac{13{,}333.3}{13{,}333.3 + 60{,}000.0} \\
&= \dfrac{13{,}333.3}{73{,}333.3} \\
&= 0.1818 \approx 0.18
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Morphology prediction from the phase diagram:**

Since $f_{PS} \approx 0.18 < 0.20$, the SBS triblock self-assembles into **polystyrene spheres dispersed in a continuous polybutadiene matrix** — exactly the morphology observed in commercial Kraton-type TPEs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Because the PS phase is the minority component forming rigid spherical domains, the material behaves as a physically crosslinked elastomer at room temperature (PS glass transition $\approx 100\ ^\circ$C), yet can be melt-processed at elevated temperatures (PB glass transition $\approx -90\ ^\circ$C). This is the structural origin of thermoplastic elastomeric behaviour.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Synthetic Method

| Desired Block Type | Compatible Method | Reason |
|:--|:--|:--|
| PS-*b*-PB (non-polar, vinyl) | Living Anionic | Clean, narrow PDI, no chain transfer |
| PS-*b*-PMMA (different polarities) | ATRP / RAFT / Click Coupling | Anionic cross-initiation between PS⁻ and MMA fails |
| PEO-*b*-PPO-*b*-PEO (Pluronics) | Sequential Anionic (EO, PO on KOH) | Industrial, water-soluble surfactants |
| PDMS-*b*-PMMA (silicone-acrylic) | Anionic → ATRP (macroinitiator route) | Two-stage method |
| PIB-*b*-PS (polyisobutylene) | Living Cationic → Anionic | PIB⁺ cannot initiate styrene directly |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Copolymer Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef monomer fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef block fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    M1[Styrene Monomer M1]:::monomer
    M2[Butadiene Monomer M2]:::monomer
    M3[Isoprene Monomer M3]:::monomer

    InitA[Initiator: n-BuLi]:::step
    PolyA[Polystyryl Anion PS-]:::block
    AddB[Sequential Addition of M2]:::step
    PolyAB[PS-b-PB- Living Anion]:::block
    Term[Termination with CH3OH]:::step

    AB[Diblock PS-b-PB]:::product
    ABA[SBS Triblock]:::product
    SIS[SIS Triblock]:::product

    M1 --> PolyA
    InitA --> PolyA
    PolyA --> AddB
    M2 --> AddB
    AddB --> PolyAB
    PolyAB --> Term
    Term --> AB

    M2 --> ABA
    PolyA --> ABA
    ABA -->|Coupling with SiCl2Me2| ABA

    M3 --> SIS
    PolyA --> SIS
    SIS -->|Coupling| SIS
```

### 4.2 Microphase Separation Morphology Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef morph fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    F[f_PS Volume Fraction of Polystyrene]:::input

    F -->|f less than 0.20| S1[PS Spheres in PB Matrix]:::morph
    F -->|0.20 to 0.35| S2[PS Cylinders in PB Matrix]:::morph
    F -->|0.35 to 0.45| S3[Gyroid Bicontinuous]:::morph
    F -->|0.45 to 0.55| S4[Alternating Lamellae]:::morph
    F -->|0.55 to 0.65| S5[PB Cylinders in PS Matrix]:::morph
    F -->|f greater than 0.65| S6[PB Spheres in PS Matrix]:::morph

    S1 --> A1[Thermoplastic Elastomer]:::app
    S2 --> A2[Selective Membranes]:::app
    S3 --> A3[Photonic Crystals]:::app
    S4 --> A4[Mechanical Blending Compatibiliser]:::app
    S5 --> A5[High Modulus TPE]:::app
    S6 --> A6[Impact Modifier in PS]:::app
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SBS Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef s1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef s2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef s3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef s4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    S1[Step 1: Purify styrene and butadiene. Remove moisture and oxygen using n-BuLi scavenging and high-vacuum line]:::s1
    S2[Step 2: Initiate styrene with n-BuLi in cyclohexane at 50 C to form living PS-Li]:::s2
    S3[Step 3: Add butadiene. Living PS-Li propagates to give PS-b-PB-Li living diblock]:::s3
    S4[Step 4: Add coupling agent dimethyldichlorosilane to link two PS-b-PB chains into PS-b-PB-b-PS triblock SBS]:::s4
    S5[Step 5: Terminate with methanol. Precipitate, wash, dry, and characterise by GPC and NMR]:::s1
    S6[Final Product: SBS Thermoplastic Elastomer]:::s3

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a block copolymer. How is it different from a graft copolymer?*

**Model Answer:**
A **block copolymer** is a macromolecule in which two or more distinct homopolymer segments are joined end-to-end along the same chain in a linear sequence (e.g., AB, ABA, ABC). Each segment is a long continuous run of one monomer.

**Difference from graft copolymer:** In a **graft copolymer**, chains of one monomer are attached as **side branches** onto the main backbone of the second monomer. Hence, graft copolymers have a **branched (comb-like) architecture**, whereas block copolymers are typically **linear** (though star-block forms also exist). Block copolymers also exhibit more uniform microphase separation due to their well-defined architecture. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List any three commercially important block copolymers and mention one application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Block Copolymer | Structure | Application |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **SBS** (Kraton) | Polystyrene-*b*-Polybutadiene-*b*-Polystyrene | Thermoplastic elastomer for shoe soles, asphalt modification |
| **SEBS** | Polystyrene-*b*-Poly(ethylene-co-butylene)-*b*-Polystyrene | Automotive interior parts, soft-touch grips, medical tubing |
| **PEO-*b*-PPO-*b*-PEO** (Pluronics / Poloxamers) | Polyethylene oxide-*b*-Polypropylene oxide-*b*-Polyethylene oxide | Pharmaceutical drug solubilisation, cosmetics, industrial cleaners |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *(a) Describe the mechanism of synthesis of an SBS triblock copolymer by living anionic polymerisation. (7 marks) (b) Discuss the microphase separation behaviour of SBS and explain why it behaves as a thermoplastic elastomer. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Synthesis of SBS by Living Anionic Polymerisation — 7 marks**

1. **Purification (1 mark):** Styrene, butadiene, and cyclohexane solvent are rigorously purified to remove moisture, oxygen, and protic impurities. *n*-BuLi is used as a scavenger to titrate residual impurities.

2. **Initiation of Block A (1 mark):** *n*-Butyllithium (*n*-BuLi) initiator is added to purified styrene. The C–Li bond attacks the vinyl group, generating a polystyryl carbanion (PS⁻ Li⁺).
   $$ \text{BuLi} + n\,\text{Styrene} \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-(\text{St})_n^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} $$

3. **Propagation of Block A (1 mark):** The carbanion propagates until styrene is fully consumed. The chain remains "living" — no termination occurs. Temperature is maintained around 40–60 °C.

4. **Sequential Addition of Butadiene (1 mark):** Purified butadiene is injected. The polystyryl anion initiates butadiene polymerisation at the 1,2- and 1,4-positions, forming a living PS-*b*-PB⁻ Li⁺ diblock.

5. **Coupling to form Triblock (2 marks):** A bifunctional coupling agent, typically **dimethyldichlorosilane (SiMe₂Cl₂)** or **1,2-dibromoethane**, joins two PS-*b*-PB⁻ Li⁺ chains to yield the ABA triblock:
   $$ 2\,\text{PS-}b\text{-PB}^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} + \text{SiMe}_2\text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{PS-}b\text{-PB-SiMe}_2\text{-PB-}b\text{-PS} + 2\,\text{LiCl} $$

6. **Termination and Recovery (1 mark):** Methanol is added to neutralise residual carbanions. The polymer is precipitated in a non-solvent (e.g., isopropanol), filtered, washed, vacuum-dried, and characterised by GPC, ¹H-NMR, and DSC.

**(b) Microphase Separation and Thermoplastic Elastomeric Behaviour — 7 marks**

1. **Incompatibility of Blocks (1 mark):** PS and PB are highly immiscible (Flory–Huggins $\chi$ parameter is large and positive), so they phase-separate at the molecular level.

2. **Microdomain Formation (2 marks):** Because the PS blocks are covalently linked to PB blocks, macroscopic phase separation is prevented. The system forms **microphase-separated domains** — typically PS spheres, cylinders, or lamellae dispersed in a continuous PB matrix, depending on PS volume fraction.

3. **Physical Crosslinking (2 marks):** The glassy PS domains (Tg ≈ 100 °C) act as **physical crosslinks** that lock the chain ends in place at room temperature. The rubbery PB matrix (Tg ≈ –90 °C) provides elastic recovery.

4. **Why "Thermoplastic" (1 mark):** Upon heating above the PS glass transition (~100 °C), the PS domains soften and flow, allowing the material to be melt-processed (extrusion, injection moulding) like a thermoplastic.

5. **Why "Elastomer" (1 mark):** On cooling, the PS domains re-harden, restoring elasticity. This reversible physical crosslinking avoids the irreversible covalent crosslinks of vulcanised rubber, making SBS fully **recyclable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 / Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022]** *(a) Explain the living anionic polymerisation mechanism. Why is it called "living"? (7 marks) (b) Compare anionic, ATRP, and RAFT methods for block copolymer synthesis. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Living Anionic Polymerisation — 7 marks**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Living polymerisation is a chain-growth process in which chain transfer and chain termination are absent, so all chains initiate simultaneously and propagate without termination.

2. **Initiation (1 mark):** A strong base/nucleophile (e.g., *n*-BuLi, Na-naphthalenide) generates a carbanion:
   $$ \text{BuLi} + M \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-M^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} $$

3. **Propagation (1 mark):** The carbanion attacks the next monomer, forming a new carbanion that continues propagation:
   $$ \text{Bu}-M_n^{-} + M \longrightarrow \text{Bu}-M_{n+1}^{-} $$

4. **No Termination (2 marks):** Because carbanions cannot undergo disproportionation, combination, or chain transfer to monomer/solvent under ideal conditions, the chains remain "alive" indefinitely until an external terminating agent is added.

5. **Why "Living" (1 mark):** The active chain-end persists after monomer is consumed. New monomer can be added later to continue growth, enabling **block copolymer synthesis by sequential monomer addition**. This was demonstrated by **Michael Szwarc in 1956**.

6. **Features (1 mark):** Narrow molecular weight distribution ($M_w/M_n \approx 1.0–1.1$), predictable $M_n$ from stoichiometry, ability to form block copolymers, stars, and cyclic polymers.

**(b) Comparison of Anionic, ATRP, and RAFT — 7 marks**

| Feature | Anionic | ATRP | RAFT |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Active species | Carbanion | Carbon radical (dormant) | Thiocarbonylthio radical (CTA) |
| Monomer scope | Limited (vinyl, styrenic, diene) | Wide (styrenes, acrylates, methacrylates) | Very wide (most radical monomers) |
| Functional group tolerance | Poor (sensitive to electrophiles) | Good | Excellent |
| Reaction conditions | Ultrapure, inert atmosphere, low T | Ambient, tolerant of impurities | Ambient, tolerant of impurities |
| Metal catalyst | Not required (Li, Na, K) | Cu(I)/Cu(II) complex with ligand | Not required |
| PDI control | Excellent (1.0–1.1) | Good (1.1–1.3) | Good (1.1–1.3) |
| Block copolymer route | Sequential monomer addition | Macroinitiator or "one-pot" chain extension | Macro-CTA chain extension |
| Industrial examples | SBS, Kraton, Pluronics | Specialty block copolymers, drug conjugates | Specialty polymers, biomedical |

(1 mark for each comparative row × 5 key rows = 5 marks; 2 marks for concluding statement.)

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Anionic polymerisation is **historically dominant for industrial block copolymers** (SBS, SIS, Pluronics) due to its excellent molecular weight control, but it is restricted to a narrow monomer range. ATRP and RAFT are **modern controlled radical methods** that allow access to a much wider variety of functional block copolymers suitable for biomedical and electronics applications, at the cost of slightly broader PDI and more complex reagent systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "block" with "random" copolymer:** Many students write random sequences when asked to draw block copolymers. Always show **continuous segments** (e.g., AAAAA–BBBBB), not alternating units.
> 2. **Missing "living" nature in anionic mechanism:** Students often write a generic radical mechanism. KTU examiners specifically require emphasis on **absence of termination** and the **sequential monomer addition** strategy.
> 3. **Forgetting coupling agent for SBS:** SBS is **not** made by three-step sequential addition alone; it requires a **bifunctional coupling agent** (SiMe₂Cl₂) or a difunctional initiator to form the central B block. Two living PS-*b*-PB chains are joined.
> 4. **Wrong volume fraction calculation:** Using **weight fraction** instead of **volume fraction** for morphology prediction. Always divide molar mass by density to get volume.
> 5. **Ignoring inert atmosphere requirement:** Anionic polymerisation will fail instantly in the presence of water or CO₂. Examiners often award a mark for mentioning **Schlenk-line/high-vacuum technique**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Block copolymer** = linear sequence of distinct homopolymer segments (AB, ABA, ABC, or multiblock).
- **Living anionic polymerisation** (Szwarc, 1956) is the classical route: no termination, no chain transfer, controlled $M_n$ by $[M]/[I]$ stoichiometry.
- **Sequential monomer addition** is the key technique: Block A is grown first, then Block B is initiated by the living anionic end of A.
- **SBS triblock** uses a **bifunctional coupling agent** (e.g., SiMe₂Cl₂) to join two PS-*b*-PB diblocks.
- **Microphase separation** occurs due to thermodynamic incompatibility ($\chi N > 10.5$); morphology depends on the **volume fraction** $f$ of one block.
- **Pluronics (PEO-*b*-PPO-*b*-PEO)** are made by sequential anionic polymerisation of ethylene oxide and propylene oxide on a KOH/HOPO initiator system.
- **Thermoplastic elastomers (TPEs)** rely on hard-block domains (e.g., PS) acting as **physical crosslinks** that are reversible on heating — enabling recyclability unlike vulcanised rubber.
- **Modern alternatives:** ATRP, RAFT, NMP, RCM, click chemistry, and mechanochemical methods allow access to functional/monomer-incompatible block combinations.
- **Volume fraction calculation** must use $\rho$ (density) — not weight — to predict morphology.
- **Industrial examples to remember:** SBS, SIS, SEBS, Kraton, Pluronics, Pebax (PA-*b*-PEO).
- **Application domains:** footwear, asphalt modification, drug delivery, compatibilisers, adhesives, photonic crystals, membranes, and nanopatterning.
- **Key KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** "living polymerisation," "sequential monomer addition," "microphase separation," "physical crosslinking," "Flory–Huggins interaction parameter," "thermoplastic elastomer," "macroinitiator," "chain transfer agent (CTA)," and "Szwarc initiator."
